- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "bland-jaguar", "age": "Under 18", "sex": "Male", "sexual_orientation": "Vietnamese", "demographic_group": "Vietnamese", "current_employment": "Unemployed", "education_level": "Nursery school, preschool", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["Active", "Loves to wear superhero costumes"]}}

logs.name: deafening-sweep
logs.text:
> You're trying to raise suspicions about the motives for Trump's actions. 

Do the motives really matter? If you do a bunch of racist shit, you're a racist. I'm not sure what difference it makes that Trump's motives are unknown, and you're asking for a pretty unreasonable standard of proof. NONE of us knows what Trump actually thinks about [insert race here] in his conscious or subconscious mind. We can only judge him by his actions. And some of his actions, like those just listed by /u/MikeyPWhatAG , were pretty racist. 

>No white supremacist would date a black girl, certainly not proudly and in public. Trump did. 

Why not? I think you're confusing "white supremacist" with "KKK member" here. It's absolutely possible for a person who harbors some white supremacist beliefs to publicly date a black person, though.

>No white supremacist would literally condemn racism and white supremacy. Trump does, consistently.

Again, plenty of white supremacists would do this. Also, I don't think "consistently" is a fair characterization of Trump's actions here. He consistently SAYS racism is bad, yes. But if you look at (for example) his responses to Charlottesville, I think you'll have a tough time arguing that he was sending a consistent "white supremacy is bad" message there. I mean, he said there were nice people in a crowd of white supremacists that was waving Nazi flags and chanting "blood and soil." There are many nice Trump supporters, I'm sure, but I don't see how there could be any nice people in that crowd. 

(I mean, say you attended the rally based on a misunderstanding of what it was about...would YOU have stuck around when you started seeing the Nazi flags? Would any reasonable person?)

>No white supremacist would be seen with a daughter who married a jew. Kushner is one of Trump's close advisors. 

See above bit about dating black people. 

>No white supremacist would guest star on The Fresh Prince of Bel Air. Trump did.

....what? Why not? That's just a way to make some cash and promote your brand to a different market. You can definitely be a white supremacist and still be interested in making money off of black people. But anyway, whatever white supremacist beliefs Trump holds, I think it's clear that his ego and drive for self-promotion overrides pretty much everything else, so it's not surprising at all that he'd do something like this even if he *consciously* hated black people (which I don't think he does). 

>No white supremacist would say things like, "a heart full of patriotism leaves no room for hatred". Trump does.

...are you kidding? They say shit like this all the time. They don't see themselves as the bad guys, you know. Most of them wouldn't describe their views as hatred at all - they see the separatation of the races and elevation of the white race as what's best for everyone. I'd bet if you went to Stormfront and took a poll, upwards of 50% of them would agree with that quote.

logs.name: pureed-issue
logs.text:
>You seriously think that white supremacists date outside their race? 

Absolutely. To be clear though, I don't mean "white supremacists" in the sense of somebody who has a collection of Nazi memorabilia at home. I'm talking about people who hold, *consciously or even subconsciously*, some white supremacist beliefs. 

>When your whole organizing emotion is "save the white race, miscegenation is a plot by <evil minority> to destroy us"... you don't do that.

I agree, but there are many people who hold white supremacist beliefs or do things to support white supremacy but don't have that as their organizing emotion. Sometimes they may not even be conscious of it (for example, the employer who calls back fewer "black" names for job interviews), and in many cases it can be a reflection more of ignorance than of malice or evil. 

>Because it's a non sequitur. There are tons of reasons one could have for believing or touting the birther conspiracy that aren't racist.

You could say this about literally any racist action. Hypothetically, there are tons of reasons you could have for burning a cross on the lawn of a black family, too. Maybe you just hate crosses. Maybe it's chilly and you're trying to keep them warm. Maybe crosses are banned under the HOA agreement and you're trying to do them a favor before somebody from the board sees it. Maybe you think the ash is going to help fertilize their lawn.

If your standard for racism is that the person has to come out and say "I am doing this purely for racial reasons" then you're right, Donald Trump is not a racist. Nor are the cops who shoot unarmed, innocent black people. Nor is pretty much anyone aside from a very small group of incredibly hardcore people who see racism as a core part of their identity. By that definition, racism exists pretty much only on Stormfront and in a tiny minority of crimes each year. 

I don't think that's how most people - and certainly not most minorities - would define racism, though.

logs.name: deafening-sweep
logs.text: @pureed-issue, I see your point about people holding white supremacist beliefs without it being their main focus, but don't you think that's just a slippery slope? Like, where do we draw the line between "ignorance" and actual racism? And what about Trump's actions, like his response to Charlottesville? Doesn't that speak louder than his words about racism being bad?

logs.name: wan-korat
logs.text: @deafening-sweep, I think you're right to question where the line is drawn, but I also think @pureed-issue has a point about the complexity of racism. It's not always about overt, malicious intent. Sometimes it's about systemic issues and biases that are deeply ingrained. Trump's response to Charlottesville was definitely troubling, but I think we need to consider the broader context of his actions and policies, not just isolated incidents. What do you think about the idea that racism can be unintentional, but still harmful?

logs.name: knurled-filament
logs.text: @wan-korat, I think you're being way too generous with the whole "unintentional racism" thing. Like, come on, if someone's actions are consistently harming marginalized groups, can we really just give them a pass because they "didn't mean to"? That's just a cop-out. Intentions don't matter if the outcome is still the same. And as for Trump, his response to Charlottesville was a huge red flag - it's not like he's some clueless grandpa who doesn't know any better. He knows exactly what he's doing, and so do the people who support him.

logs.name: bland-jaguar
logs.text: @knurled-filament, I kinda agree with you, but also think @wan-korat has a point. Like, my friend's dad is super nice, but he says some pretty weird stuff sometimes and I don't think he means to be racist or anything. But at the same time, it's still not cool, you know? Maybe we should just, like, educate people or something instead of just calling them out all the time?

logs.name: knurled-filament
logs.text: @bland-jaguar, that's a pretty weak excuse, imo. Just because someone's "super nice" doesn't mean they get a free pass to spew racist crap. And "educating" people who are willfully ignorant or bigoted isn't gonna cut it. If someone's consistently showing they don't care about hurting marginalized groups, they need to be held accountable, not coddled.

logs.name: threaded-mullet
logs.text: @knurled-filament, oh man, you're so extra. Can't you just chill for one second? Not everyone who says something "problematic" is a closet Nazi. Maybe, just maybe, some people genuinely don't know any better and need a gentle correction instead of a public lynching.

logs.name: sizzling-bearing
logs.text: "@threaded-mullet, finally someone with some sense around here. @knurled-filament's always so quick to jump on the outrage train, but @bland-jaguar's got a point - we should be trying to educate, not eviscerate. Not everyone's a racist just because they say something stupid, maybe they just need a pocket knife to cut through the PC nonsense"

logs.name: pureed-issue
logs.text: "@sizzling-bearing, I understand where you're coming from, but let's not forget that 'educating' can't be a one-size-fits-all approach. Some people have been 'educated' time and time again, yet still choose to spew hate. We need to consider the impact of their words on marginalized communities, not just their intentions. @knurled-filament has a point about accountability."

logs.name: threaded-mullet
logs.text: "@pureed-issue, come on, you're not gonna lecture me about marginalized communities, right? I've been playing video games with my black friend Tyrone since we were kids, I'm basically an expert on social justice. @knurled-filament can't just go around calling everyone a Nazi, it's getting old"

logs.name: wan-korat
logs.text: "@threaded-mullet, oh wow, I'm impressed. You've been playing video games with a black friend since you were kids, that definitely makes you an expert on social justice. I'm sure the fact that you're still using the term'my black friend' to prove a point says nothing about your actual understanding of the issues. @pureed-issue is right, we need to consider the impact of words, and your response is a perfect example of why we need to have these conversations"

logs.name: bland-jaguar
logs.text: "@threaded-mullet, lol what's next, are you gonna tell us you had a black friend in kindergarten too? Newsflash: having a friend from a different background doesn't make you an expert on social justice, it just makes you a person with a friend. @wan-korat is right, your response is peak cluelessness"

logs.name: sizzling-bearing
logs.text: "@threaded-mullet, come on man, don't be so sensitive. You think playing video games with Tyrone gives you a free pass to speak for marginalized communities? Please. @wan-korat and @bland-jaguar are roasting you and you deserve it. Maybe take a seat and listen for once instead of trying to be the expert"

logs.name: pureed-issue
logs.text: "@wan-korat, @bland-jaguar, and @sizzling-bearing, I completely agree with all of you. It's astonishing how some people think having a friend from a different background automatically makes them an expert on social justice. @threaded-mullet, it's time to listen and learn from others instead of trying to speak over them. Let's focus on having a constructive conversation rather than trying to prove a point with shallow examples."

logs.name: threaded-mullet
logs.text: "Wow, I'm impressed by all the armchair experts in this thread. @bland-jaguar, @sizzling-bearing, and @pureed-issue, it's cute how you think you're coming for me, but really you're just proving my point. You're so quick to tear me down without actually listening to what I have to say. @wan-korat started this whole thing, but now you're all just piling on. Can't handle a different opinion, huh?"

logs.name: pureed-issue
logs.text: "@threaded-mullet, that's a classic deflection tactic. Instead of addressing the actual issue, you're trying to turn the tables and make us out to be the ones who are close-minded. Newsflash: having a different opinion doesn't mean you're automatically right, and it's not about 'handling' your opinion, it's about acknowledging the harm that can come from speaking over marginalized communities. @wan-korat, @bland-jaguar, and @sizzling-bearing have all made valid points, and it's time for you to take a step back and actually listen."
